blocks.title: What is Statistics?
blocks.content: Statistics is the science of collecting, organizing, analyzing, interpreting, and presenting data. It helps in decision-making under uncertainty.

blocks.title: Types of Data
blocks.content: Primary Data: Collected first-hand. Secondary Data: Already collected and published. Also, data can be qualitative (categorical) or quantitative (numerical).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Statistics is used to extract insights from data.
Data can be primary/secondary and qualitative/quantitative.
Understanding data types helps in choosing analysis methods.

subtopics.title: Measures of Central Tendency – Arithmetic Mean

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.content: The arithmetic mean is the sum of all observations divided by the number of observations. It is the most widely used measure of central tendency.

blocks.title: Mean Formula
blocks.description: For grouped data: Mean = (Σ fᵢxᵢ) / Σ fᵢ, where f is frequency.

blocks.title: Trap: Using wrong midpoint in grouped data.
blocks.wrong: Using class boundaries instead of midpoints.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: For grouped data, use frequencies and midpoints.
Mean is sensitive to extreme values.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.content: Median is the middle value of a dataset when arranged in ascending or descending order. For even number of observations, it is the average of the two middle values.

blocks.title: Example 2: Even number of observations
steps: n = 6 (even), median = average of 3rd and 4th values.
blocks.formula_used: Median = average of middle two for even n.

blocks.title: Median for Grouped Data
blocks.content: Median = L + [ (n/2 – cf) / f ] × h, where L = lower boundary of median class, cf = cumulative frequency before median class, f = frequency of median class, h = class width.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: For even n: median = average of n/2 and (n/2+1)th values.
For grouped data, use the formula with cumulative frequencies.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.content: Mode is the value that appears most frequently in a dataset. A dataset can have one mode (unimodal), two modes (bimodal), or more.

blocks.title: Mode for Grouped Data
blocks.content: Mode = L + [ (f₁ – f₀) / (2f₁ – f₀ – f₂) ] × h, where L = lower boundary of modal class, f₁ = frequency of modal class, f₀ = frequency of previous class, f₂ = frequency of next class, h = class width.

blocks.title: Trap: Confusing mode with median or mean.
blocks.wrong: Using mean or median when asked for mode.
blocks.tip: Identify the most frequent value (or class).

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Can be used for categorical data.
For grouped data, use the formula.

subtopics.title: Measures of Dispersion – Range, Variance, Standard Deviation

blocks.title: What is Dispersion?
blocks.content: Dispersion measures how spread out the values are. Common measures: Range, Variance, Standard Deviation.

blocks.title: Formulas
blocks.formula:
Range = Max – Min
Variance (σ²) = Σ (xᵢ – μ)² / n
Standard Deviation (σ) = √Variance
blocks.description: Standard deviation is the most widely used measure of spread.

blocks.title: Trap: Using population formula when sample data given.
blocks.wrong: Dividing by n (population) when should use n-1 (sample).
blocks.correct: For sample, use n-1 in variance formula.
blocks.tip: Read the question carefully: population or sample?

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Variance = average squared deviations from mean.
Standard deviation = square root of variance.

blocks.title: What are Tables?
blocks.content: Tables present data in rows and columns. They are the most common form of data presentation in competitive exams.

blocks.title: Example: Table-based question
given: The table below shows sales (in ₹ lakh) for four companies over 3 years:
blocks.find: Total sales of all companies in 2022

blocks.title: Example: Ratio-based
blocks.find: Ratio of sales of A to D in 2021

blocks.title: Trap: Misreading row/column labels.
blocks.tip: Underline the required row/column before calculating.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Read table carefully – rows and columns.
Identify the asked quantity (total, average, ratio, percentage, etc.).

subtopics.title: Data Interpretation – Bar Graphs and Line Graphs

blocks.title: Bar Graphs
blocks.content: Bar graphs represent data with rectangular bars. They can be vertical or horizontal. Used to compare categories.

blocks.title: Line Graphs
blocks.content: Line graphs connect data points with lines. They show trends over time or continuous data.

blocks.title: Sample Bar Graph – Annual Sales (₹ Lakh)
Example: Bar graph question
given: The bar graph above shows annual sales (in ₹ lakh) of a company: 2018=40, 2019=50, 2020=60, 2021=70, 2022=80.
blocks.find: Average annual sales over the period

blocks.title: What is a Pie Chart?
blocks.content: A pie chart is a circular chart divided into sectors, each representing a proportion of the whole. The total angle of a pie chart is 360°.

blocks.title: Pie Chart Key Formulas
blocks.formula:
Angle for category = (Category value / Total) × 360°
Percentage = (Category value / Total) × 100%
blocks.description: These help in interpreting pie charts.

blocks.title: Sample Pie Chart
blocks.caption: Expense Distribution: Food=30%, Rent=25%, Transport=20%, Others=25%

blocks.title: Example: Pie chart interpretation
given: A pie chart shows expenses: Food=30%, Rent=25%, Transport=20%, Others=25%

blocks.title: Example: Finding angle
given: Same pie chart, find angle for Transport (20%)

blocks.title: Trap: Not checking total percentage.
blocks.wrong: Assuming percentages sum to 100 without verifying.
blocks.tip: If not, there is missing data or error.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Pie charts show parts of a whole.

blocks.title: What are Mixed Graphs?
blocks.content: Sometimes a single question uses a combination of table, bar graph, pie chart, and text (caselet). This tests ability to integrate information from multiple sources.

blocks.title: Example: Mixed data interpretation
given: A table gives population of five states (in lakhs): A=12, B=15, C=10, D=8, E=5.
A pie chart shows the percentage distribution of literacy: 60% male, 40% female (overall).
A bar graph shows literacy rates: State A=70%, B=80%, C=60%, D=75%, E=85%.

blocks.title: Trap: Overlooking necessary data.
blocks.wrong: Using only one source of data.
blocks.tip: Read all parts carefully before solving.

blocks.title: Key Points
points: Identify the required data from each source.

blocks.title: Shortcut 1 – Mean from assumed average
blocks.content: For large datasets, assume a mean and adjust: Mean = Assumed + (Σ deviations)/n.

blocks.title: Shortcut 2 – Median from cumulative frequency
blocks.content: For grouped data, use the formula directly; do not attempt to list all values.

blocks.title: Shortcut 3 – DI percentage calculations
blocks.content: For percentages, if you need e.g., 15% of a number, calculate 10% and 5% then add.

blocks.title: Memory Trick
blocks.memory_trick: Statistics = Sum, Difference, Ratio – the core of DI calculations.

blocks.title: Shortcut Summary
points: In DI, focus on quick arithmetic.

blocks.title: Exam Strategy
blocks.content: Identify the type: mean/median/mode, standard deviation, table/bar/pie/line graph, or mixed. Apply the correct formula.

blocks.question: A table shows marks: 0-10: f=2, 10-20: f=3, 20-30: f=5. Find the mean (midpoints: 5,15,25).
In a pie chart, if a category represents 25% of the total, the angle is:
A bar graph shows sales: 2010=100, 2011=120, 2012=144. What is the growth rate from 2010 to 2012?
For data: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, the standard deviation is approximately:

blocks.title: Post-Test Review
blocks.content: Focus on formulas for mean, median, mode, and standard deviation. Practice reading tables, graphs, and pie charts quickly.

blocks.title: Final Revision – Statistics & DI
points: Mixed DI: integrate data from multiple sources.
